title: Investigation of nonlocal transport associated with the orbital Hall effect in Ti
authors: Keitaro Takashina, Naoki Yano, Asahi Oe, Mari Taniguchi, Shuto Kimura, Kazuya Ando
abstract: We investigate nonlocal transport in single-layer Ti Hall bars to explore signatures of orbital-current transport driven by the orbital Hall effect. Despite the negligible spin Hall effect in Ti, we observe a finite nonlocal resistance in the single-layer Ti Hall bar and study its dependence on the central channel width. Finite-element simulations show that the measured signal contains a sizable Ohmic bypass contribution. However, the bypass contribution is strongly suppressed at small channel widths and cannot fully account for the observed nonlocal resistance even when variations in the Ti resistivity are taken into account. Our results therefore suggest an additional nonlocal contribution distinct from the Ohmic bypass background, which may be associated with orbital transport driven by the orbital Hall effect in Ti.
summary: This paper investigates the origin of nonlocal resistance in titanium Hall bars. By comparing experimental data with finite-element simulations, the authors demonstrate that the signal cannot be explained by simple geometric leakage (Ohmic bypass), pointing toward the presence of the orbital Hall effect.
main_problem: The study aims to determine whether the observed nonlocal resistance in Ti Hall bars is caused by an Ohmic bypass effect or by orbital-current transport driven by the orbital Hall effect (OHE).
main_result: The experimental nonlocal resistance remains significantly higher than the simulated Ohmic bypass contribution, especially in the small-width regime, suggesting an additional contribution from the orbital Hall effect.
method: The researchers performed nonlocal resistance measurements on Ti Hall bars and used finite-element simulations (COMSOL) to model the current density distribution and the Ohmic bypass effect.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of single-layer Ti (30-nm-thick) Hall bars with a 4-nm-thick SiO2 cap, featuring a central channel with varying widths (80 to 180 nm).
key_observables: Nonlocal resistance (Rnl), nonlocal voltage (Vnl), and current density distribution.
important_parameters: Central channel width (W), channel length (L), Ti resistivity, and terminal widths.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the spin Hall effect in Ti is negligible and uses the Laplace equation to estimate the simplified bypass contribution.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the measurement geometry, COMSOL current density simulation, and SEM image of the device; Fig 2 compares measured Rnl vs W with simulated bypass contributions; Fig 3 shows a parameter sweep of Ti resistivity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of OHE in Ti, describes the device fabrication and measurement setup, presents the experimental results, compares these results to Ohmic bypass simulations, and concludes with evidence for OHE-driven transport.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Measured nonlocal resistance Rnl as a function of the central channel width W. The gray circles represent the ex- perimental data. The dashed curve represents the calculated bypass contribution using Eq. (1), which assumes negligible terminal widths (TWs) with respect to L. The solid curve shows the simulated bypass contribution accounting for fi- nite TWs using a Ti resistivity of 70 µΩcm determined from sheet-resistance measurements of a reference film (a Ti film fabricated under the same sputtering conditions on a different substrate).

FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the nonlocal measurement geom- etry for orbital transport. A charge current jc is injected through the left terminal along the y axis, which generates an orbital current jL flowing along the x axis in the central channel via the OHE. The orbital current is converted into a charge current along the y axis through the inverse OHE, giving rise to a nonlocal voltage Vnl detected at the right terminals. (b) Representative current-density distribution in the Ti Hall bar calculated using COMSOL Multiphysics, ex- plicitly taking into account the finite terminal widths. The color scale and arrows represent the magnitude and local flow direction of the current density,...

FIG. 3. Colored curves show simulated simulated bypass con- tribution as a function of the central channel width W for Ti resistivities ranging from 30 µΩcm to 90 µΩcm in steps of 10 µΩcm. Experimental data are also plotted for compari- son. The simulation shows that the bypass signal is strongly suppressed as W decreases, whereas the experimental values remain systematically larger than the simulated bypass con- tribution in the small-W regime.
